In a previous chapter, we talked about how anger could easily distort your ability to have a productive conversation. Another powerful motivator, or obstacle, is fear. Being able to ease someone's fears could be the difference between signing that new multi-million dollar client and not signing them. Just like with anger, you want to interrupt the amygdala's reaction to reel or perceive threats as quickly as possible. The quicker that we can clear the obstacles, the quicker that we can generate feelings of trust, safety, well-being, and agreement. When we label these fears, we bring them out of the dark and bathe them in sunlight. Once these fears have been acknowledged, the negative reactions from your counterpart will begin to soften. While I promise you that you'll be shocked at how suddenly someone's language turns from worry to optimism, I don't want you to think that the road is always cleared immediately. You might feel like you're getting nowhere. Don't get demoralized. I guarantee you that you just need to dig deeper for the motivator. That's why labels are so powerful and transformative to conversations. Labels help uncover and identify the primary emotion driving almost all of our counterparts' behavior. Once you find that primary emotion, their fears will diffuse. If you're ever trying to sell someone something or on something, uncover what their objections lead to. Each one acts as a clue that can help you narrow in on the true source of their reluctance. Instead of being seen as something to overcome, see them as ways to establish rapport. Think of them as a counter-offer in disguise. Another way to reduce someone's fear or anxieties by doing what I like to call an accusations order. Let me ask you this. Have you ever given your friends some bad news or some form of criticism by prefacing with something like, I don't want this to sound harsh or no offense but, I don't want this to sound harsh or no offense but,
